annotations.label: Integer

anchor.snippet: An integer is the number zero

annotations.note: The integer type with notation ℤ is the basic object of the article (type itself comes from Lean core).

annotations.label: Negative integers

anchor.snippet: The negations or additive inverses of the positive natural numbers are referred to as negative integers

annotations.note: Negation/additive inverse is provided by the additive group structure, but 'negative integers' is not defined as a named subset.

annotations.label: Chain of subsets N ⊂ Z ⊂ Q ⊂ R

anchor.snippet: which in turn is a subset of the set of all rational numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib relates these via coercion ring homomorphisms (Int.cast, Rat.cast) as distinct types, not as literal subset inclusions.

annotations.label: Z is countably infinite

anchor.snippet: Like the set of natural numbers, the set of integers

annotations.note: The Denumerable instance for ℤ states exactly that ℤ is in bijection with ℕ (countably infinite).

annotations.label: Integer as real without fractional component

anchor.snippet: An integer may be regarded as a real number that can be written without a fractional component

annotations.note: fract_intCast shows the fractional part of a cast integer is zero, capturing the 'no fractional component' idea but not a full characterization.

annotations.label: Integers vs non-integers

anchor.snippet: For example, 21, 4, 0, and −2048 are integers

annotations.note: These are illustrative numeric examples with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Smallest group and ring containing N

anchor.snippet: The integers form the smallest group and the smallest ring containing the natural numbers

annotations.note: ℤ being initial in CommRingCat captures 'smallest ring', but the Grothendieck-group 'smallest group containing ℕ' framing is not directly formalized.

annotations.label: Rational integers

anchor.snippet: integers are sometimes called rational integers

annotations.note: This is alternative terminology with no separate Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Rational integers are rational algebraic integers

anchor.snippet: (rational) integers are algebraic integers that are also rational numbers

annotations.note: ℤ being integrally closed (rationals integral over ℤ lie in ℤ) holds via this instance, formalizing the content but not stated for ℤ by name.

annotations.label: Closed under addition and multiplication

anchor.snippet: closed under the operations of addition and multiplication

annotations.note: The commutative ring structure on ℤ provides total addition and multiplication operations.

annotations.label: Closed under subtraction

anchor.snippet: unlike the natural numbers, is also closed under subtraction

annotations.note: The additive group structure gives a total subtraction operation on ℤ.

annotations.label: Z is initial object in category of rings

anchor.snippet: The integers form a ring which is the most basic one

annotations.note: zIsInitial proves ℤ is the initial object of the category of commutative rings.

annotations.label: Injectivity iff characteristic zero

anchor.snippet: This unique homomorphism is injective if and only if the characteristic of the ring is zero

annotations.note: The forward direction (CharZero implies the integer cast hom is injective) is formalized, but the full iff is not a single declaration.

annotations.label: Characteristic-zero rings contain a copy of Z

anchor.snippet: every ring of characteristic zero contains a subring isomorphic to

annotations.note: Injectivity of ℤ →+* R for CharZero R yields a subring of R isomorphic to ℤ.

annotations.label: Not closed under division

anchor.snippet: since the quotient of two integers

annotations.note: No Mathlib theorem asserts that integer quotients fail to be integers.

annotations.label: Not closed under exponentiation

anchor.snippet: the natural numbers are closed under exponentiation

annotations.note: No Mathlib theorem states that ℤ fails to be closed under (negative) exponentiation.

annotations.label: Z under addition is an abelian group

anchor.snippet: under addition, is an abelian group

annotations.note: ℤ is given an AddCommGroup instance.

annotations.label: Z under addition is cyclic

anchor.snippet: It is also a cyclic group

annotations.note: An IsAddCyclic ℤ instance (generated by 1) is provided.

annotations.label: Z is the unique infinite cyclic group

anchor.snippet: is the only infinite cyclic group

annotations.note: intCyclicAddEquiv gives an isomorphism ℤ ≃+ G for any infinite cyclic additive group G.

annotations.label: Z under multiplication is a commutative monoid

anchor.snippet: under multiplication is a commutative monoid

annotations.note: ℤ is given a CommMonoid instance under multiplication.

annotations.label: Z under multiplication is not a group

anchor.snippet: not every integer has a multiplicative inverse

annotations.note: Int.isUnit_iff shows the only units are ±1, implying not every integer is invertible, but 'ℤ is not a multiplicative group' is not stated directly.

annotations.label: Z is a commutative ring with unity

anchor.snippet: together with addition and multiplication is a commutative ring with unity

annotations.note: ℤ is given a CommRing instance.

annotations.label: Z is an integral domain

anchor.snippet: The lack of zero divisors in the integers

annotations.note: ℤ is given an IsDomain instance (no zero divisors, nontrivial).

annotations.label: Z is not a field

anchor.snippet: The lack of multiplicative inverses, which is equivalent to the fact that

annotations.note: Int.not_isField proves ¬ IsField ℤ.

annotations.label: Smallest field containing Z is Q

anchor.snippet: The smallest field containing the integers as a subring is the field of rational numbers

annotations.note: ℚ is the fraction field (smallest field) of ℤ via the IsFractionRing ℤ ℚ instance.

annotations.label: Euclidean division

anchor.snippet: It is called Euclidean division

annotations.note: Characterizes the unique quotient and remainder with a = r + b*q and 0 ≤ r < |b|.

annotations.label: Z is a Euclidean domain (hence PID)

anchor.snippet: is a Euclidean domain

annotations.note: ℤ is given a EuclideanDomain instance (from which PID follows).

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of arithmetic

anchor.snippet: any positive integer can be written as the products of primes in an essentially unique way

annotations.note: Uniqueness (up to permutation) of prime factorization of naturals; existence is prod_primeFactorsList.

annotations.label: Z is totally ordered without bounds

anchor.snippet: is a totally ordered set without upper or lower bound

annotations.note: ℤ has a LinearOrder (total order); NoMaxOrder/NoMinOrder (unboundedness) follow from its strict ordered ring structure.

annotations.label: Positive and negative integers

anchor.snippet: An integer is positive if it is greater than zero

annotations.note: Positivity/negativity is expressed as 0 < n / n < 0 via the order; there is no dedicated named subset definition.

annotations.label: Order compatible with operations

anchor.snippet: The ordering of integers is compatible with the algebraic operations

annotations.note: The strict ordered ring instance encodes compatibility of the order with addition and multiplication.

annotations.label: Z is an ordered ring

anchor.snippet: together with the above ordering is an ordered ring

annotations.note: ℤ is given an IsStrictOrderedRing instance.

annotations.label: Z characterized as well-ordered ordered abelian group

anchor.snippet: The integers are the only nontrivial totally ordered abelian group whose positive elements are well-ordered

annotations.note: This order-theoretic uniqueness characterization of ℤ is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Traditional construction of integers

anchor.snippet: integers are often intuitively defined as the union of the (positive) natural numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib uses Lean core's inductive definition of ℤ, not a union-of-naturals-and-negatives construction in Mathlib/.

annotations.label: Piecewise arithmetic operations

anchor.snippet: The traditional arithmetic operations can then be defined on the integers in a piecewise fashion

annotations.note: The piecewise definition of operations under the traditional construction is not used in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Integers as equivalence classes of pairs

anchor.snippet: The integers can thus be formally constructed as the equivalence classes of ordered pairs of natural numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib does not construct ℤ as the Grothendieck quotient of ℕ×ℕ; it uses the core inductive type.

annotations.label: Equivalence relation on pairs

anchor.snippet: we define an equivalence relation

annotations.note: The equivalence relation on ℕ×ℕ used for the pair construction is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Addition and multiplication of classes

anchor.snippet: Addition and multiplication of integers can be defined in terms of the equivalent operations on the natural numbers

annotations.note: Operations defined on equivalence classes of pairs are not part of Mathlib's ℤ construction.

annotations.label: Negation of a class

anchor.snippet: The negation (or additive inverse) of an integer is obtained by reversing the order of the pair

annotations.note: Negation via reversing pair order is specific to the unused pair construction.

annotations.label: Subtraction via additive inverse

anchor.snippet: subtraction can be defined as the addition of the additive inverse

annotations.note: sub_eq_add_neg states a - b = a + -b in any subtraction monoid, including ℤ.

annotations.label: Standard ordering on classes

anchor.snippet: The standard ordering on the integers is given by

annotations.note: The ordering defined on equivalence classes of pairs is not used by Mathlib.

annotations.label: Canonical representative of each class

anchor.snippet: Every equivalence class has a unique member that is of the form

annotations.note: Canonical representatives of the pair-construction equivalence classes are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Examples of class arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Some examples are

annotations.note: These are worked examples of the pair construction with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Multiple constructions of signed integers

anchor.snippet: There exist at least ten such constructions of signed integers

annotations.note: This is a meta-mathematical remark about alternative constructions, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Z is countably infinite

anchor.snippet: The set of integers is countably infinite

annotations.note: The Denumerable instance for ℤ encodes countable infiniteness via a bijection with ℕ.

annotations.label: Pairing of integers with naturals

anchor.snippet: An example of such a pairing is

annotations.note: intEquivNat is an explicit equivalence ℤ ≃ ℕ.

annotations.label: Cardinality of Z is aleph-null

anchor.snippet: More technically, the cardinality of

annotations.note: Cardinal.mk_int proves #ℤ = ℵ₀.

annotations.label: Bijection

anchor.snippet: is called a bijection

annotations.note: Function.Bijective defines a bijection as an injective and surjective function.
